content.problemStatement:
You are given a sequence w of integers. A mismatch is any such pair of neighbouring elements of sequence w[i] and w[i+1], that w[i]>w[i+1]+1.
As long as there is any mismatch, you solve it by swapping the mismatching numbers. Given an input sequence, calculate one of the possible output mismatch-less sequences obtained by successively solving mistmatches by swapping.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 100000) on its own line, followed by a line with N non‑negative integers w1, w2, …, wN separated by spaces.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N integers – any sequence that can be obtained by repeatedly swapping adjacent mismatching pairs until no mismatches remain. The numbers must be separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case already satisfies w[i] ≤ w[i+1] + 1, so it is printed unchanged. In the second test case a mismatch exists (4 > 1+1). Sorting yields 1 2 3 4, but any mismatch‑free permutation is allowed; the sample output shows one possible result after a sequence of swaps.
The original array has a mismatch at positions 3 (2 > 0+1). Sorting the array gives 0 2 3 4 5, which satisfies the condition for every adjacent pair.
All adjacent differences are 0, therefore no mismatches exist; the original sequence is already valid.

solution.approach:
A mismatch exists when w[i] > w[i+1] + 1. Swapping adjacent elements can realise any permutation, therefore we can freely reorder the array. Any non‑decreasing order trivially satisfies w[i] ≤ w[i+1] ≤ w[i+1] + 1, so no mismatches remain. The algorithm is:
1. Read the array.
2. Scan once to check whether the current array already satisfies w[i] ≤ w[i+1] + 1 for all i. If it does, output it unchanged.
3. Otherwise sort the array in ascending order and output the sorted array. The sorted array is guaranteed to be mismatch‑free.
The scan is O(N) and sorting is O(N log N). This meets the limits for N up to 100 000.
Correctness proof sketch:
- If the original array satisfies the condition, no swap is required, so outputting it is valid.
- If it does not satisfy the condition, we output a sorted array. For any i, sorted[i] ≤ sorted[i+1]; consequently sorted[i] > sorted[i+1] + 1 can never hold, so the output contains no mismatches.
- Because adjacent swaps can generate any permutation, the sorted array is reachable from the original by a sequence of allowed swaps, thus it is a possible final state.
Hence the algorithm always produces a valid answer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (dominant term is sorting). The initial scan is O(N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the array (in‑place sorting uses only O(1) extra).
